title: Jorg Flecker (Ed.): Changing Working Life and the Appeal of the Extreme Right.
abstract: This book is the end product of a project entitled ‘Socio-economic change, individual reactions and the appeal of the extreme right’ (SIREN) funded by the European Commission. The project is cross-national and cross-disciplinary combining the efforts of sociologists and social psychologists from eight European countries (Austria, Belgium, Denmark, France, Hungary, Italy and Switzerland). The stated aim of the book is to investigate ‘the interplay between the far-reaching transformation of working life in recent years and the growing appeal of political right-wing populism and extremism in Europe’ (p. 1). The researchers involved in the project have collected new empirical evidence both in the form of in-depth interviews with workers who have experienced changes at the workplace and in the form of large-scale telephone surveys in each of the eight countries. The results of these efforts are presented in this edited volume. The first three chapters as well as the introduction and the conclusion present the overarching questions and findings. The remaining chapters are studies of each of the eight countries conducted by the relevant countries’ research teams. The latter eight chapters are very loosely connected to each other as the authors themselves have decided on which aspect of the main theme to study and emphasize in their own country. In the interest of space and clarity, this review will therefore focus on the chapters that present the overarching questions and evidence.
